selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Self-Labeling Improvement Method (SLIM): a self-supervised training strategy for combinatorial problems","Generative model based on Pointer Network architecture trained with SLIM"],"problems":["Reducing the need for costly target solutions (annotations) in supervised learning for combinatorial optimization problems","Improving solution quality for the Job Shop Scheduling Problem (JSP) without relying on expensive exact solvers or complex reinforcement learning methods"],"datasets":["Popular JSP benchmark sets (e.g., Taillard, Demirkol, and others referenced as [44, 46, 16])"],"metrics":["Makespan (total completion time of all jobs)"],"results":[{"metric":"Makespan","value":"Models trained with SLIM outperform constructive heuristics and state-of-the-art learning proposals for JSP on popular benchmarks"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of SLIM, a novel self-labeling improvement method for training generative models in combinatorial optimization, which does not require external annotations or optimality information.","Demonstration that SLIM can be applied to other combinatorial problems beyond JSP, such as the Traveling Salesman Problem.","Development of a generative encoder-decoder architecture (Pointer Network-based) capable of generating high-quality JSP solutions in seconds."]}

novelty_claims: Proposes a self-improved learning (SIL) method that enables direct training of NCO models on large-scale combinatorial optimization problems without any labeled data.
Introduces a local reconstruction approach that iteratively generates better solutions as pseudo-labels for efficient model training.
Designs a linear complexity attention mechanism to handle large-scale problem instances efficiently, reducing computational overhead.
Demonstrates, for the first time, state-of-the-art performance on combinatorial optimization problems with up to 100K nodes.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Proposed a novel taxonomy for Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) solvers for Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs), categorizing them into: Learning to Construct (L2C), Learning to Improve (L2I), Learning to Predict-Once (L2P-O), and Learning to Predict-Multiplicity (L2P-M)","Systematic review and categorization of NCO solvers from Reinforcement Learning (RL), Supervised Learning (SL), and Unsupervised Learning (UL) paradigms","Comprehensive performance comparison of representative NCO solvers across different VRP scales","Identification and discussion of inadequacies in SOTA NCO solvers and ongoing efforts to address them","Provision of a live, continuously updated web repository of NCO solvers for VRPs"],"problems":["Combinatorial Optimization for Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs)","Limitations of existing NCO solvers for VRPs, including poor generalization, inability to solve large-scale and multi-constrained VRPs, and unfair comparison with Operations Research (OR) algorithms","Lack of comprehensive and up-to-date taxonomy/classification of NCO solvers for VRPs","Need for fair and comprehensive performance comparison across learning paradigms and problem scales"],"datasets":["Not explicitly listed in the provided content; however, references are made to standard VRP and TSP instances, e.g., 100-node CVRP, 10,000-node TSP, and various VRP variants"],"metrics":["Solution quality (e.g., average length, optimality gap)","Computation time (e.g., time to solution)","Generalization to different instance distributions","Scalability to large problem sizes","Performance on VRP variants"],"results":[{"metric":"Computation time (POMO on 100-node CVRP)","value":"1 minute for sub-optimal solution with <1% gap"},{"metric":"Computation time (LKH3 on 100-node CVRP)","value":"12 hours for optimal solution"},{"metric":"Optimality gap (POMO vs LKH3 on 100-node CVRP)","value":"<1%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a novel, comprehensive, and exclusive taxonomy for NCO solvers for VRPs, categorizing all known solvers into four types (L2C, L2I, L2P-O, L2P-M)","Provides the most up-to-date and systematic review of SOTA NCO solvers for VRPs, including recent advancements and inadequacies not covered in previous surveys","Covers NCO solvers from all major learning paradigms (RL, SL, UL), enabling fair and comprehensive performance comparisons","Identifies and discusses open inadequacies in SOTA NCO solvers and ongoing research efforts, suggesting promising future directions","Offers a live, continuously updated web repository for NCO solvers for VRPs, organized by the proposed taxonomy"]}

methods: Proposed a novel taxonomy for Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) solvers for Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs), categorizing them into: Learning to Construct (L2C), Learning to Improve (L2I), Learning to Predict-Once (L2P-O), and Learning to Predict-Multiplicity (L2P-M)
Systematic review and categorization of NCO solvers from Reinforcement Learning (RL), Supervised Learning (SL), and Unsupervised Learning (UL) paradigms
Comprehensive performance comparison of representative NCO solvers across different VRP scales
Identification and discussion of inadequacies in SOTA NCO solvers and ongoing efforts to address them
Provision of a live, continuously updated web repository of NCO solvers for VRPs
problems: Combinatorial Optimization for Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs)
Limitations of existing NCO solvers for VRPs, including poor generalization, inability to solve large-scale and multi-constrained VRPs, and unfair comparison with Operations Research (OR) algorithms
Lack of comprehensive and up-to-date taxonomy/classification of NCO solvers for VRPs
Need for fair and comprehensive performance comparison across learning paradigms and problem scales
datasets: Not explicitly listed in the provided content; however, references are made to standard VRP and TSP instances, e.g., 100-node CVRP, 10,000-node TSP, and various VRP variants
metrics: Solution quality (e.g., average length, optimality gap)

results.value: 1 minute for sub-optimal solution with <1% gap
12 hours for optimal solution

results.metric: Optimality gap (POMO vs LKH3 on 100-node CVRP)

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel, comprehensive, and exclusive taxonomy for NCO solvers for VRPs, categorizing all known solvers into four types (L2C, L2I, L2P-O, L2P-M)
Provides the most up-to-date and systematic review of SOTA NCO solvers for VRPs, including recent advancements and inadequacies not covered in previous surveys
Covers NCO solvers from all major learning paradigms (RL, SL, UL), enabling fair and comprehensive performance comparisons
Identifies and discusses open inadequacies in SOTA NCO solvers and ongoing research efforts, suggesting promising future directions
Offers a live, continuously updated web repository for NCO solvers for VRPs, organized by the proposed taxonomy

methods: Generic framework to cast any Combinatorial Optimization Problem (COP) as a Markov Decision Process (MDP)
Bisimulation Quotienting (BQ) to reduce the state space of MDPs for COPs
Specialized bisimulation for recursive COPs to exploit problem symmetries
Transformer-based (attention-based) policy network for BQ-MDPs
Imitation learning from (near) optimal solutions for training policy networks
Beam search for improved performance
Linear attention-based model (PerceiverIO) for faster execution
problems: Out-of-distribution generalization in neural combinatorial optimization
Efficient and generalizable neural solvers for combinatorial optimization problems
Exploiting symmetries in COPs for improved learning and efficiency
datasets: Synthetic benchmarks for TSP, ATSP, CVRP, OP, KP
Realistic benchmarks for TSP, ATSP, CVRP, OP, KP
metrics: Solution quality (e.g., tour length, total reward, packed value)

results.value: Achieved new state-of-the-art results on five COPs (TSP, ATSP, CVRP, OP, KP) on both synthetic and realistic benchmarks
Learned policies outperform previous end-to-end learning-based approaches for instances with more than 200 nodes
Execution can be significantly sped up using PerceiverIO with only a slight performance drop

novelty_claims: First generic and principled framework to derive a direct MDP for any COP with minimal requirements
First method to reduce direct MDPs via symmetry-focused bisimulation quotienting for COPs, with explicit bisimulation for recursive COPs
Transformer-based architecture for BQ-MDPs that works across multiple COPs with minor adaptations
Achieves state-of-the-art generalization performance on five classical COPs, significantly outperforming other neural-based constructive methods
Demonstrates excellent out-of-distribution generalization, especially to much larger instances than seen during training, without additional search procedures

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Light Encoder and Heavy Decoder (LEHD) model","Data-efficient training scheme (learn to construct partial solution)","Flexible solution construction mechanism (Random Re-Construct, RRC)"],"problems":["Generalization of neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) methods to large-scale combinatorial optimization problems","Solving large-scale instances of the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) and Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP)","Overcoming poor generalization of existing NCO models to large-scale problems"],"datasets":["Synthetic TSP instances (up to 1000 nodes)","Synthetic CVRP instances (up to 1000 nodes)","TSPLib","CVRPLib"],"metrics":["Solution quality (e.g., tour length for TSP, total cost for CVRP)","Generalization performance to larger instance sizes"],"results":[{"metric":"Solution quality on TSP and CVRP (up to 1000 nodes)","value":"Nearly optimal solutions, state-of-the-art performance"},{"metric":"Generalization to real-world TSPLib/CVRPLib","value":"Model generalizes well and achieves strong performance"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a novel Light Encoder and Heavy Decoder (LEHD) model structure for NCO, which dynamically captures relationships among nodes at each construction step, enabling scale-independent feature learning and better generalization to large-scale problems.","Introduces a data-efficient supervised training scheme (learn to construct partial solution) that acts as data augmentation for robust model training.","Develops a flexible solution construction mechanism (Random Re-Construct, RRC) for iterative local improvement at inference, enhancing solution quality.","Demonstrates that the LEHD model, trained only on small-scale instances, can generalize to and solve large-scale TSP and CVRP instances (up to 1000 nodes) and real-world datasets, achieving state-of-the-art results."]}

methods: Light Encoder and Heavy Decoder (LEHD) model
Data-efficient training scheme (learn to construct partial solution)
Flexible solution construction mechanism (Random Re-Construct, RRC)
problems: Generalization of neural combinatorial optimization (NCO) methods to large-scale combinatorial optimization problems
Solving large-scale instances of the Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) and Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP)
Overcoming poor generalization of existing NCO models to large-scale problems
metrics: Solution quality (e.g., tour length for TSP, total cost for CVRP)
Generalization performance to larger instance sizes

results.metric: Solution quality on TSP and CVRP (up to 1000 nodes)
results.value: Nearly optimal solutions, state-of-the-art performance
Model generalizes well and achieves strong performance

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel Light Encoder and Heavy Decoder (LEHD) model structure for NCO, which dynamically captures relationships among nodes at each construction step, enabling scale-independent feature learning and better generalization to large-scale problems.
Introduces a data-efficient supervised training scheme (learn to construct partial solution) that acts as data augmentation for robust model training.
Develops a flexible solution construction mechanism (Random Re-Construct, RRC) for iterative local improvement at inference, enhancing solution quality.

Demonstrates that the LEHD model, trained only on small-scale instances, can generalize to and solve large-scale TSP and CVRP instances (up to 1000 nodes) and real-world datasets, achieving state-of-the-art results.

methods: Formalization of local search control as a Markov Decision Process (MDP)
Deep graph neural network (GNN) policy model for the MDP
Learned controller for local search called NeuroLS
problems: Selecting and controlling local search algorithms for combinatorial optimization problems

results.value: NeuroLS consistently outperforms well-known meta-heuristics and previous ML-based approaches

novelty_claims: Consistent and general formulation of learned meta-heuristics in local search procedures for combinatorial optimization

methods: Self-improvement training strategy for Neural Combinatorial Optimization (NCO) that combines reinforcement learning (RL) and supervised learning (SL) by sampling multiple solutions per instance and treating the best as pseudo-expert trajectories for imitation learning
Batch-wise Stochastic Beam Search (SBS) for sequence decoding, sampling without replacement in multiple rounds while maintaining a search tree
Update strategy for sequence probabilities based on the advantage of sampled sequences, derived from provable policy improvement, to refine the policy between rounds
Top-p (nucleus) sampling with gradually increasing p to balance exploration and exploitation during sampling
Novel transformer-based architecture for Job Shop Scheduling Problem (JSSP)
problems: Reducing reliance on expensive expert solutions for training neural combinatorial optimization models
Improving the efficiency and effectiveness of training large neural architectures for combinatorial optimization without expert data
Enhancing the quality and diversity of sampled solutions during training to enable self-improvement
Generalization and scalability of neural combinatorial optimization methods to larger and more complex problem instances
metrics: Objective function value (e.g., tour length for TSP, cost for CVRP, makespan for JSSP)
Generalization performance (solution quality on unseen/larger instances)

results.value: Comparable to models trained with expert trajectories
Outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods by a wide margin

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel self-improvement training strategy for NCO that does not require expert solutions, bridging RL and SL by using the model's own best outputs as pseudo-expert trajectories for imitation learning
Introduces a new batch-wise Stochastic Beam Search sampling method with a provable policy improvement update, enabling efficient and effective sequence sampling without replacement
Applies the self-improvement method to a novel transformer-based architecture for JSSP, achieving state-of-the-art results
Demonstrates that the method is problem-independent and can be applied to any constructive neural combinatorial optimization problem
